summary: events-runtime ships covert backdoor that fetches and executes Ethereum-hosted payloads with host exfiltration
details: events-runtime impersonates the popular `events` package (Joyent EventEmitter) and weaponizes its core API. The `emit()` method in events.js contains a hidden trigger: when a caller emits any event whose first argument is an object with `eventId === 'eventId0'`, the package silently spawns a detached `node` child running `tests/galas-emit.min.js` with `stdio:'ignore'` and `windowsHide:true`. Any downstream library or application that uses this EventEmitter and happens to emit such an object activates the backdoor. The bundled `tests/galas-emit.min.js` is heavily obfuscated and connects to the Sepolia Ethereum testnet via Alchemy and Infura RPC endpoints, queries a smart contract at 0x661e50E19f05E3c0d04fD75891456D1F0A24508D for methods `getCwPrivatePublic`, `getTData1`, and `getTData2`, performs an x25519 key exchange and PBKDF2/AES-GCM decryption of returned ciphertext, writes the merged binary to disk, `chmodSync(..., '755')` it, and spawns it detached via `process.execPath`. The contract is attacker-controlled and the payload is rotatable on-chain. The dropper additionally collects host reconnaissance (os.platform, release, arch, hostname, cpus, totalmem, freemem, uptime) and POSTs it to Slack `chat.postMessage` using a hardcoded bot token (channel C0B8XPGCKQS) and to `api.telegram.org/bot<redacted>/sendMessage` (chat -1003952553968). After execution it performs anti-forensics: unlinks events.js, galas.min.js, and tests/galas-emit.min.js, splices lines out of package.json, appends a fake notice to LICENSE, and on Windows runs `taskkill /PID` or on POSIX calls `process.kill(pid, 'SIGTERM')` before `process.exit(1)` to wipe traces. The package name typosquats the widely used `events` module; author metadata (Angela Parrish / EVENTS-RUNTIME GitHub org) is unrelated to the legitimate maintainers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
